Motion: real animal weight, grounded foot/body contact, dust/water/leaves/particles reacting naturally, no rubber limbs.
Tone: premium documentary wildlife survival scene meets premium hard-surface sci-fi realism.

cards.name: Cargo Ant

cards.videoPromptVeo: Vertical 9:16 cinematic wildlife shot, 8 seconds. Cargo Ant, a photorealistic biomechanical ant in underground colony. Organic anatomy fused with exposed precision mechanics, load-bearing exoskeleton pistons and swarm antenna mesh. IMAGE-TO-VIDEO REFERENCE LOCK: use the uploaded image as the exact design source. Preserve the same animal identity, silhouette, pose logic, visible biomechanical parts, fur/skin/scale/feather/shell coverage, colors, and environment from first frame to last frame. Cargo Ant stays in the same natural habitat shown in the reference image and performs only small believable wildlife movements: breathing, subtle head turn, eye contact with the camera, tiny joint articulation, small weight shift, gentle paw/fin/wing/body adjustment, and minor biomechanical surface motion in the already-visible mechanisms: load-bearing exoskeleton pistons and swarm antenna mesh. Do not invent a new event. Do not reveal hidden layers. Do not add or remove fur, skin, scales, feathers, shell, plates, cables, pistons, or armor. Do not make anything grow, peel, shed, melt, cover, uncover, reconfigure, vanish, appear, break, or transform. The whole video is calm premium documentary wildlife observation of the existing design. Emotional hook: One gram. Industrial strength Story mode: reference locked micro-motion. Camera: locked wildlife-documentary camera or very slow controlled push-in; stable framing, no sudden reframing, no fast zoom, no shake, no scene change. Neutral natural illumination only, realistic shadows, shallow depth of field, tactile metal and skin textures, believable animal anatomy, grounded foot/body contact. No glow, no light beams, no holograms, no magical flashes, no sci-fi energy effects. Single continuous shot, no cuts, no text, no logos, no extra creatures, no sudden event, no attack, no chase, no threat, no camouflage reveal, no transformation, no growing fur, no shedding fur, no peeling skin, no hidden mechanism reveal.

Format: vertical 9:16. Build as 5 short clips or one guided multi-shot prompt.

Camera: controlled close detail, 85mm lens feel, focus pull. Purpose: wow moment.

cards.natgeoEncounter:
NATGEO PREDATOR/PREY SERIES PROMPT
Format: vertical 9:16, cinematic wildlife documentary, 12-18 seconds, intense but non-graphic.
Primary creature: Orbit Starfish, a photorealistic biomechanical starfish in tidal pool, organic anatomy fused with exposed precision mechanics, magnetic tube feet and modular limb repair nodes.
Second biomechanical creature: a swarm of biomechanical metallic plankton shifting as one textured cloud in shallow water, naturally adapted to tidal pool, visually distinct but not stealing the main identity.
World rule: both creatures are organic-machine hybrids from the same ecosystem and share the same premium biomechanical visual language.
Story structure:
0-3s: establish tidal pool; show subtle movement from both creatures, like a real wildlife observation.
3-6s: tension builds. Orbit Starfish detects the second creature through its biomechanical upgrade, not through random motion.
6-11s: attack or chase beat. One creature lunges, ambushes, defends, dodges, or escapes. Keep the action readable and physically believable.
11-15s: the biomechanical upgrade becomes the decisive moment: Magnetic tube feet and modular limb repair nodes. Resolve with a clean freeze-frame style ending.
Emotional hook: Five arms. One strange intelligence.
Camera: long-lens wildlife documentary feeling, low angle when close, quick but controlled tracking during the chase, no chaotic camera shake.
Motion: real animal weight, grounded foot/body contact, dust/water/leaves/particles reacting naturally, no rubber limbs.
Tone: premium documentary wildlife survival scene meets premium hard-surface sci-fi realism.
Avoid: gore, blood, cruelty focus, extra random animals, broken anatomy, toy-like metal, subtitles, logos, scene teleportation, incoherent morphing.

cards.storyboard30s:
STORYBOARD 30s / MICRO-STORY
Format: vertical 9:16. Build as 5 short clips or one guided multi-shot prompt.

Reference lock for every shot: use the same creature design, same visible mechanics, same body covering, same colors, and same habitat. No body-covering changes and no new events.

SHOT 01 — 4s — Setup / reference locked micro-motion
Wide environmental shot of the same sunlit rocky scrub from the reference image. Show only calm wildlife observation and the existing terrain.
Camera: composed documentary setup with readable geography. Purpose: immediate story context.

SHOT 02 — 5s — It Notices Us
Medium shot of Thermal Monitor. The creature becomes readable as a living organism with engineered anatomy.
Camera: locked wildlife-documentary camera or very slow controlled push-in; stable framing, no sudden reframing, no fast zoom, no shake, no scene change. Purpose: direct viewer connection.

SHOT 03 — 7s — Decision
The monitor lizard commits to the scene action: small natural movement only, subtle breathing, eye-line to camera when appropriate, micro servo corrections, stable visible mechanics, no transformation.
Camera: track only as much as needed to keep the action clear. Purpose: intention and momentum.

SHOT 04 — 8s — Hook Action
The already-visible biomechanical details move subtly: Heat-reading tongue sensors and armored elbow servos. Show micro servos, contact shadows, and tactile materials. No new parts appear.
Camera: controlled close detail, 85mm lens feel, focus pull. Purpose: wow moment.

SHOT 05 — 6s — Resolution / Cover Frame
Resolve the micro-story with a memorable final image. Emotional hook: The rock moved before the lizard did.
Camera: slow dolly-in, stable frame, neutral natural illumination only, no artificial light effects. Purpose: cover frame and final retention spike.

GLOBAL QA: keep the same animal identity, silhouette, biomechanical design, color palette, and habitat across all shots. No text, no logos, no extra creatures, no passive walking loops, no repeated approach-to-camera formula, no deformed anatomy, no plastic look, no random scene changes, no sudden events, no threat, no attack, no chase, no camouflage reveal, no transformation, no growing fur, no shedding fur, no peeling skin, no mechanisms appearing or disappearing, no glow, no light beams, no holograms, no magical flashes, no sci-fi energy effects.
cards.natgeoEncounter:
NATGEO PREDATOR/PREY SERIES PROMPT
Format: vertical 9:16, cinematic wildlife documentary, 12-18 seconds, intense but non-graphic.
Primary creature: Thermal Monitor, a photorealistic biomechanical monitor lizard in sunlit rocky scrub, organic anatomy fused with exposed precision mechanics, heat-reading tongue sensors and armored elbow servos.
Second biomechanical creature: a swarm of tiny biomechanical organisms with visible texture and joint movement from the same ecosystem, naturally adapted to sunlit rocky scrub, visually distinct but not stealing the main identity.
World rule: both creatures are organic-machine hybrids from the same ecosystem and share the same premium biomechanical visual language.
Story structure:
0-3s: establish sunlit rocky scrub; show subtle movement from both creatures, like a real wildlife observation.
3-6s: tension builds. Thermal Monitor detects the second creature through its biomechanical upgrade, not through random motion.
6-11s: attack or chase beat. One creature lunges, ambushes, defends, dodges, or escapes. Keep the action readable and physically believable.
11-15s: the biomechanical upgrade becomes the decisive moment: Heat-reading tongue sensors and armored elbow servos. Resolve with a clean freeze-frame style ending.
Emotional hook: The rock moved before the lizard did.
Camera: long-lens wildlife documentary feeling, low angle when close, quick but controlled tracking during the chase, no chaotic camera shake.
Motion: real animal weight, grounded foot/body contact, dust/water/leaves/particles reacting naturally, no rubber limbs.
Tone: premium documentary wildlife survival scene meets premium hard-surface sci-fi realism.
Avoid: gore, blood, cruelty focus, extra random animals, broken anatomy, toy-like metal, subtitles, logos, scene teleportation, incoherent morphing.
